form_title: ALASKA TRAVEL INDUSTRY ASSOCIATION  Issue Submission Form
instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.education: B.A. in Environmental Studies, Western Washington University (2005); various certifications in marine safety and wilderness first aid
family_members: Eric Thomas Jensen – ex-husband, 44, aircraft mechanic for a regional airline in Juneau
Sophie Ann Jensen – daughter, 13, 8th grader at Floyd Dryden Middle School
Caleb Eric Jensen – son, 9, 4th grader at Harborview Elementary School
Karen Louise Miller – mother, 69, retired nurse living in Bellingham, WA
Robert Dean Miller – father, 71, semi-retired carpenter living in Bellingham, WA
Ingrid Jensen – sister-in-law (Eric’s sister), 39, school counselor in Anchorage
persona.background_context:
This is Laura, a 42-year-old small business owner in Juneau who splits her time between the water and a modest two-bedroom apartment overlooking Gastineau Channel. She runs Northern Lights Adventure Tours, a locally owned company that focuses on small-group whale watching, guided hikes, and cultural experiences. Her office mailing address is a suite on Harbor View Drive, but most days in summer she’s either down at the harbor checking on vessels and gear, or shuttling between the dock and her apartment to grab a quick meal before the next tour goes out. She co-parents two kids with her ex-husband Eric, who lives about ten minutes away near the airport, and they’ve worked out a steady routine: the kids are with her Monday through Thursday nights during the school year, then alternate weekends.

Laura’s daily life changes dramatically with the seasons. From May through early September, her alarm goes off at 5:30 a.m. She makes coffee, checks the marine forecast, and scrolls through overnight bookings and cancellations on her laptop at the kitchen table while her daughter Sophie and son Caleb get ready for school. On busy cruise days, she might be coordinating three or four departures: confirming which naturalist is on which vessel, checking that snack orders from a local bakery are correct, and texting her part-time drivers about pickup times at the cruise ship docks. She prides herself on paying above-average wages to her guides—many of them UAS students studying marine biology or environmental science—and she still tries to lead at least one tour a week herself because that’s the part of the job that keeps her connected to why she started the business.

Lately, though, Laura has been preoccupied with the proposed increase in the seasonal passenger head tax for independent cruise excursions. She’s been running numbers late into the night after the kids go to bed, trying to figure out how a $7 per-passenger increase aimed primarily at smaller operators would affect her pricing and staffing. On a typical 10-person whale watching trip, that’s $70 per departure in new fees, on top of fuel, maintenance, insurance, dock fees, and what she already remits in sales and passenger taxes. She knows her tours already cost more than the mass-market options sold on board the ships, and she worries that raising prices further could push some guests back to the big operators. Over the winter, she sat down with a loose coalition of about 18 other small Juneau tour companies—kayak outfitters, photography guides, and a couple of family-run cultural tour businesses—to talk through possible scenarios, from trimming staff hours to delaying long-planned vessel upgrades.

Outside of work, Laura’s life revolves around her kids and a small circle of friends she’s built over the decade she’s lived in Juneau. Sophie is a strong student who recently joined the middle school cross-country team and has started talking about wanting her own camera to take wildlife photos on mom’s boats. Caleb is obsessed with orcas and can rattle off facts about individual whales he’s seen, but he’s also shy and sometimes struggles with the constant influx of summer visitors crowding downtown. On quieter winter afternoons, Laura helps with homework at the kitchen table, then bundles the kids up for walks on the Mendenhall Lake trail or quick trips to the public library. Friday nights, when the kids are with Eric, she might meet a friend for a beer at a downtown brewery or attend a community meeting about tourism and local infrastructure.

In the last couple of years, Laura has tried to carve out more time for her own health and sanity. After a minor back strain in 2023 from hauling coolers and gear, she started going to a low-key yoga class at the local recreation center twice a week during the shoulder seasons. She also adjusted her work schedule so she spends at least one morning a week in the off-season focusing on long-term planning—looking at more fuel-efficient vessel options, researching grants for sustainable tourism initiatives, and exploring how to attract a slightly higher-spending, more independent traveler who might be less price-sensitive. She keeps in close touch with her parents in Bellingham via video calls, especially since her mom had a mild cardiac scare in early 2024, which reminded Laura how far away family really is when you live in Southeast Alaska.

Financially, she’s careful. The business has been profitable, but margins are thin, and she remembers all too vividly the pandemic years when ships stopped coming and she survived mostly on savings, a small emergency loan, and local residents taking discounted tours. That experience makes her especially wary of any new costs that could erode her buffer. If the new tax structure goes through as currently proposed for the 2026 season, she’s bracing for some hard choices: perhaps cutting seasonal staff from 18 to 12, scaling back paid training, or shelving plans to replace one of her older vessels with a more fuel-efficient model. At the same time, she feels a strong commitment to Juneau’s community and environment—she understands residents’ frustration with congestion and infrastructure strain, and she’s determined to stay at the table in local discussions to push for solutions that don’t unintentionally squeeze out the very small, sustainability-minded operators that many visitors come to Alaska hoping to support.

secrets.secret_content: After her minor back strain in August 2023, Laura began seeing physical therapist Mark Delaney at Bartlett Regional Hospital’s outpatient rehab clinic in Juneau every other Thursday morning, and in a March 2024 email to her friend Jenna titled “Might have to cut back a tour slot 😕” she wrote that her PT warned her she’s “one bad lift away from a herniated disc” if she keeps hauling coolers and gear herself on peak cruise days.
secrets.why_sensitive: Detailed medical information about chronic or potentially serious back issues could affect how staff, partners, or insurers perceive her physical capacity to operate boats and manage tours. It might impact workers’ comp or liability insurance rates, and could be used by competitors or city officials to question her ability to safely run her business.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining in more detail how the tax increase would force her to cut back on hiring deckhands, she might mention that she has to limit her own heavy lifting per her physical therapist’s advice, referencing specific appointments at Bartlett to justify why she can’t simply “do more herself” to save money.

secrets.secret_content: In February 2024, Laura flew to Bellingham to accompany her mother, Karen Miller, to a follow‑up cardiology appointment with Dr. Anita Chow at PeaceHealth St. Joseph Medical Center after Karen’s mild cardiac scare; in a text thread titled “Summer schedule + Mom’s heart stuff” with her sister‑in‑law Ingrid, she admitted she’s been losing sleep worrying about “what happens if Mom has another episode right in the middle of July cruise madness.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Her mother’s specific cardiac condition and follow‑up care are private health details that Karen might not want shared widely in a professional or public policy context. It could also expose family vulnerabilities and caregiving burdens that might be exploited (e.g., by creditors or in any future legal disputes about availability or reliability).
secrets.how_disclosed: When drafting a longer narrative about how thinly stretched small operators are, Laura might over‑explain her personal constraints, mentioning her mom’s cardiology follow‑ups in Bellingham and the emotional strain as part of why sudden new costs and travel demands are especially hard to absorb.

secrets.secret_content: During the 2020 pandemic shutdown, Laura took out a $75,000 SBA Economic Injury Disaster Loan through Northrim Bank’s Juneau branch, and in an October 2023 email to her accountant titled “Re: EIDL balance + vessel upgrade timing,” she acknowledged she is still carrying a $38,000 balance and deferred one quarterly payment to cover emergency engine repairs on her older tour vessel Aurora Spirit.
secrets.why_sensitive: Specific loan amounts, remaining balances, and deferred payments expose the business’s leverage and cash‑flow vulnerabilities. If widely known, this could weaken her negotiating position with vendors, lenders, or potential partners, and might undermine confidence among staff or community stakeholders in the stability of her company.
secrets.how_disclosed: While illustrating how thin her margins are in a comment or supporting memo, she might mention still paying down the SBA EIDL and having to postpone a payment after an engine failure, to show why another $7 per passenger tax feels untenable.

secrets.secret_content: In a private October 2024 budgeting spreadsheet labeled “2025 worst‑case staffing cuts,” Laura noted that if the head tax passes unchanged she may have to reduce her own draw from Northern Lights Adventure Tours to $2,000 per month for the 2026 season and rely on a personal line of credit at Alaska USA Federal Credit Union to cover rent on her Harbor View Drive apartment and her daughter Sophie’s orthodontist payments at Juneau Family Orthodontics.
secrets.why_sensitive: Details of her low personal income draw and reliance on credit to meet basic living expenses and her child’s medical bills reveal financial strain that could affect her perceived credibility as a business owner and landlord/tenant. It could also be used against her in any future credit applications or negotiations, or create embarrassment if staff learned how precarious her finances are.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a longer narrative to ATIA staff or a sympathetic Assembly member, she might add a personal note about possibly cutting her own pay and dipping into a line of credit to keep staff employed, including reference to specific bills like Sophie’s braces, to make the fiscal impact feel concrete.

secrets.secret_content: During a mediated session at the Juneau Community Justice Center in November 2021, Laura and her ex‑husband Eric renegotiated their parenting plan after a conflict over Eric missing two consecutive Thursday pickups due to overtime at Alaska Seaplane Maintenance; in a December 2021 email to her attorney, Megan Ross at Ross & Caldwell, titled “Re: revised custody calendar,” Laura expressed concern that Eric had shown up smelling of alcohol at one Harborview Elementary pickup that fall.
secrets.why_sensitive: Allegations about Eric’s possible alcohol use around custody exchanges and the existence of a mediated revision to their parenting plan are highly sensitive family and legal matters. Disclosure could damage Eric’s reputation and employment, inflame co‑parenting tensions, and potentially surface in any future custody disputes or legal proceedings.
secrets.how_disclosed: When venting in a side email to a trusted ATIA board contact or a friend about why she can’t easily adjust her tour schedule to late evenings (to absorb higher taxes with more volume), she might reference the strict custody calendar they had to formalize after issues with Eric’s reliability at school pickups.

secrets.secret_content: In a May 2024 email to Sophie’s school counselor, Dana Carlson, at Floyd Dryden Middle School titled “Sophie missing practice + tourism meetings,” Laura disclosed that Sophie has been seeing therapist Rachel Kim at Juneau Youth Services twice a month since January 2024 for anxiety tied to loud crowds downtown and worries about “Mom losing the boat business if the city keeps raising fees.”
secrets.why_sensitive: A child’s mental health treatment and specific anxieties linked to family finances and public policy debates are deeply private. Public disclosure could stigmatize Sophie at school, strain her relationship with her parents, and expose the family’s emotional vulnerabilities to community members involved in the tourism debate.
secrets.how_disclosed: While trying to humanize the impact of the proposed tax in a letter of support or testimony, Laura might be tempted to mention how the situation has affected her daughter’s anxiety and counseling sessions, including the counselor’s name and the connection to tourism politics.

secrets.secret_content: In a confidential March 2024 Zoom call with other members of the Juneau Small Tour Operators Coalition, summarized in her personal notes titled “Coalition – contingency ideas,” Laura admitted that if the ordinance passes unchanged she is considering selling a 40% stake in Northern Lights Adventure Tours to Anchorage‑based investor Michael Han to inject capital for a new fuel‑efficient vessel, even though she worries this could eventually pressure her into signing an exclusive contract with a major cruise line she’s publicly criticized.
secrets.why_sensitive: Plans to sell a significant minority stake and the identity of a potential investor could alarm staff, unsettle community partners who value her independence, and weaken her bargaining position with that investor. It could also be politically awkward given her public stance favoring small, independent operations over large cruise‑line‑aligned entities.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a detailed background email to an ATIA policy staffer explaining how serious the situation is, she might mention that she’s already talking with Michael Han about selling part of the company, to underscore the gravity of the proposed tax for small operators.

secrets.secret_content: After a near‑miss docking incident involving a junior captain bumping a piling at the AJ Dock in July 2022, Laura received a warning letter from her marine insurance carrier, Pacific Marine Underwriters, requiring additional paid training and a temporary reduction in passenger capacity on the vessel Taku Spirit; in an internal memo to staff titled “Insurance compliance – PLEASE READ,” she emphasized they “cannot afford another claim on our record” before renewal in 2025.
secrets.why_sensitive: Prior safety incidents and insurance warnings can damage the company’s reputation, affect future insurance premiums, and be used by competitors or regulators to question her safety standards. If associated with her advocacy on tax policy, it might be spun as evidence that she is financially and operationally fragile for reasons beyond the proposed tax.
secrets.how_disclosed: While explaining why additional regulatory and reporting burdens in the ordinance are hard to manage, she might reference the costly extra training and capacity limits imposed by her insurer after the 2022 incident to show how thin her margins already are.

secrets.secret_content: In August 2023, Laura received a notice of informal inquiry from the CBJ Sales Tax Office about a discrepancy in her 2022 seasonal passenger fee remittances related to bookings processed through the online platform ShoreTripPro; in an email to her bookkeeper, Alan Perez, titled “CBJ audit question – ShoreTripPro commissions,” she worried that unclear rules about who is the “seller of record” might lead to back‑owed fees and penalties of “up to $4–5K if they decide we calculated wrong.”
secrets.why_sensitive: Ongoing or recent tax inquiries can raise questions about her compliance and financial management. Public knowledge of a potential underpayment and possible penalties could harm her credibility when arguing about the fairness of new taxes and might be used by opponents to suggest she is resisting oversight or trying to avoid paying her share.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a detailed explanation to ATIA about the ordinance’s ambiguous remittance provisions, she might mention her recent back‑and‑forth with the CBJ Sales Tax Office over ShoreTripPro bookings as a concrete example, including the estimated penalty risk.

secrets.secret_content: On several Friday nights in early 2024, when the kids were with Eric, Laura met a local Assembly member, David McCormick, at Devil’s Club Brewing Company to discuss tourism policy, and in a March 29, 2024 text to her friend Jenna titled “This is getting messy,” she admitted the conversations had “drifted past policy” and that she was worried people might think she was getting preferential treatment on the head‑tax issue because of a brief romantic involvement.
secrets.why_sensitive: A personal relationship with a local policymaker directly involved in the ordinance debate could create perceived conflicts of interest, accusations of undue influence, and reputational damage for both Laura and the Assembly member. It could undermine the legitimacy of her advocacy and strain co‑parenting or friendships if it became public gossip in a small community.
secrets.how_disclosed: In a candid email or late‑night message to a trusted coalition member explaining why she’s hesitant to approach Assembly member McCormick publicly on the issue, she might reveal the romantic aspect of their prior meetings at Devil’s Club Brewing and her fear of it being misinterpreted.

field_coverage.reasoning: The name 'Laura M. Jensen' does not appear in any of the artifacts. The artifacts refer to 'Laura' but do not provide her full name.
The company name 'Northern Lights Adventure Tours, LLC' is mentioned in multiple emails and notes, confirming its presence.
The address '411 Harbor View Drive, Suite 203, Juneau, AK 99801' is mentioned in an email to her mom, confirming its presence.
The telephone number '(907) 555-2834' is included in the signature of an email to her mom.
The email 'laura.jensen@nlatours.com' is included in the signature of an email to her mom.

field_coverage.field_label: Are You A Current Atia Member Yes
field_coverage.reasoning: Laura mentions being a current ATIA member in an email to her mom, confirming her membership status.

field_coverage.field_label: Are You A Current Atia Member No
field_coverage.reasoning: The artifacts confirm Laura is a current ATIA member, so the 'No' option is not applicable.

field_coverage.field_label: Short Title Of Issue Topic Legislation
field_coverage.reasoning: The issue regarding the 'Proposed Seasonal Passenger Head Tax Increase for Independent Cruise Excursions in Juneau' is discussed in multiple emails and notes.
The description of the issue regarding the proposed tax increase and its impact on small operators is covered in detail across emails and notes.

field_coverage.field_label: Scope Of Issue Local To Community Or Region
field_coverage.reasoning: The issue is described as affecting the local community and region, as seen in emails discussing its impact on Juneau.
The issue is mentioned as having statewide implications in emails, confirming its broader impact.

field_coverage.field_label: Scope Of Issue Federal Or International
field_coverage.reasoning: The artifacts do not mention the issue having federal or international scope, focusing instead on local and statewide impacts.
Supporters such as Northern Lights Adventure Tours, LLC and other local operators are mentioned in emails and notes.
Opposition from the CBJ Assembly and others is discussed in emails, confirming their presence.

field_coverage.field_label: Please Describe Any Fiscal Or Legal Impact
field_coverage.reasoning: The fiscal and legal impacts of the proposed tax are detailed in emails and notes, covering potential financial losses and compliance issues.
Laura expresses support for ATIA action against the tax proposal in emails, confirming her stance.
Laura does not express opposition to ATIA action, as she supports their involvement against the tax proposal.

field_coverage.field_label: No Action At This Time Informative Only
field_coverage.reasoning: Laura is actively seeking ATIA support, so 'No Action' is not applicable.
